qualifying-columns-across-tables.label: Qualifying Columns Across Tables
qualifying-columns-across-tables.summary: Use table-qualified column references so SQL and readers know which source each value comes from.

try_qualifying_columns_across_tables_ske.title: Disambiguate Enrollment and Student Columns
try_qualifying_columns_across_tables_ske.prompt: Return enrollment id, student name, and term from `enrollments` joined to `students`. Qualify every source column and give the two displayed identifiers readable aliases.

help.concept: Qualification uses `table.column` to identify the source; an output alias changes only the displayed heading.
help.hint_1: Use full table names in `SELECT` and in the `ON` condition.
help.hint_2: Keep this a two-table query and order by the enrollment identifier.

try_qualifying_columns_across_tables_ske.solutionCode:
SELECT enrollments.id AS enrollment_id,
       students.name AS student_name,
       enrollments.term
FROM enrollments
INNER JOIN students
  ON enrollments.student_id = students.id
ORDER BY enrollments.id;

try_qualifying_columns_across_tables_ske.title: Disambiguate Course and Department Columns
try_qualifying_columns_across_tables_ske.prompt: Return course id, course title, and department name from `courses` joined to `departments`. Qualify every selected column and use stable output headings.

help.concept: Qualified source columns keep a two-table query unambiguous even when both tables contain an `id` column.
help.hint_2: Alias the displayed columns as `course_id`, `course_title`, and `department_name`.

try_qualifying_columns_across_tables_ske.solutionCode:
SELECT courses.id AS course_id,
       courses.title AS course_title,
       departments.name AS department_name
FROM courses
INNER JOIN departments
  ON courses.department_id = departments.id
ORDER BY courses.id;

single-choice-1.title: Purpose of Qualifying Columns
single-choice-1.prompt: Why is it important to qualify column names in SQL queries involving multiple tables?
single-choice-1.hint: Think about how SQL distinguishes between columns with the same name in different tables.

help.concept: Qualifying columns helps SQL distinguish between columns with the same name in different tables.
help.hint_1: Consider what happens if two tables have a column with the same name.
help.hint_2: Qualifying columns prevents ambiguity in SQL queries.

options.a: To prevent ambiguity when columns have the same name in different tables.
options.b: To make the query run faster.
options.c: To reduce the size of the database.
options.d: To ensure the database schema is correct.

single-choice-2.title: Using Table Aliases
single-choice-2.prompt: What is the main benefit of using table aliases in SQL queries?
single-choice-2.hint: Consider how aliases affect the readability and length of SQL queries.

help.concept: Table aliases provide shorthand references for table names, making queries more readable.
help.hint_1: Aliases help shorten the query without losing clarity.
help.hint_2: Using aliases can make complex queries easier to manage.

options.a: They make queries shorter and easier to read.
options.b: They increase the execution speed of queries.
options.c: They allow for more tables to be joined.

multi-choice-1.title: Identifying Qualified Columns
multi-choice-1.prompt: Which of the following are examples of qualified column names? Select all that apply.
multi-choice-1.hint: Qualified column names include the table name as a prefix.

help.concept: Qualified column names include the table name to avoid ambiguity.
help.hint_1: Look for column names that are prefixed with a table name.

multi-choice-2.title: Benefits of Using Aliases
multi-choice-2.prompt: What are the benefits of using table aliases in SQL queries? Select all that apply.
multi-choice-2.hint: Think about how aliases affect query readability and management.

help.concept: Table aliases simplify queries by providing shorthand references for table names.
help.hint_1: Aliases can make complex queries easier to read and write.
help.hint_2: They help manage queries with multiple tables.

drag-reorder-1.title: Order of SQL Clauses
drag-reorder-1.prompt: Arrange the SQL clauses in the correct order for a basic SELECT query.
drag-reorder-1.hint: Read each piece and arrange them in the order the statement should be understood.

help.concept: The pieces should form a valid statement in a logical order.
help.hint_1: Start with the piece that introduces the idea or action.
help.hint_2: Place dependent pieces after the part they describe or complete.

fill-blank-choice-1.title: SQL Aliases Syntax
fill-blank-choice-1.prompt: Fill in the blank: To create an alias for a table in SQL, use the keyword `[blank1]`.
fill-blank-choice-1.hint: Read the sentence around the blank and decide what role the missing word plays.

help.concept: The blank should be filled with the term that makes the statement accurate.
help.hint_1: Look at the words before and after the blank to infer what kind of term is needed.
help.hint_2: Pick the choice that completes the statement with the clearest meaning.

fill-blank-choice-1.template: To create an alias for a table in SQL, use the keyword `[blank1]`.

sketch0.title: Understanding Qualified Columns
sketch0.bodyMarkdown:
When joined tables both contain columns such as `id` or `name`, an unqualified reference can be ambiguous. Write the source explicitly:

```sql
SELECT enrollments.id, students.name
FROM enrollments
INNER JOIN students
  ON enrollments.student_id = students.id;
```

`enrollments.id` means the enrollment record. `students.id` would mean the student record. Qualification answers **where the value comes from**.

sketch1.title: Source qualification and output aliases solve different problems
sketch1.bodyMarkdown:
A qualified reference identifies the source column. An output alias controls the result-table heading:

```sql
SELECT enrollments.id AS enrollment_id,
       students.id AS student_id
FROM enrollments
INNER JOIN students
  ON enrollments.student_id = students.id;
```

Both expressions use a source table and column. The aliases make the two output headings distinct. Table aliases are introduced in the next topic; here, keep the full table names visible while you practice the distinction.
